title: The Mom &Amp; Pop Store

jokes.text: No baron has ever owned a LeBaron.
jokes.setup: Car names are so stupid, aren't they?
jokes.punchline: No baron has ever owned a LeBaron.
jokes.explanation: Sharp observational humor about car naming conventions. Perfect economy and a genuinely clever connection between aristocratic titles and car marketing.

jokes.text: Or the Ford LTD. LTD. Limited. It's a limited edition. What did they make, 50 million of those? Yes, it's limited to the number we can sell.
jokes.setup: The Ford LTD stands for Limited
jokes.punchline: Yes, it's limited to the number we can sell
jokes.explanation: Classic Seinfeld deconstruction of marketing language, building logically to absurd conclusion about corporate doublespeak.

jokes.text: You know how they'll do that? The Integra. Oh, integrity? No, Integra.
jokes.setup: When they try and mangle a positive word into a car name
jokes.punchline: Oh, integrity? No, Integra
jokes.explanation: Continues the car naming theme with wordplay, though less inspired than the baron joke.

jokes.text: Well, I hope it's not a 'lemona.' Or you'll be hearing from my 'lawya.'
jokes.setup: The Supra or the Impreza
jokes.punchline: I hope it's not a 'lemona.' Or you'll be hearing from my 'lawya.'
jokes.explanation: Playful wordplay creating fictional car names that sound like mangled words, with escalation to 'lawyer.'

jokes.text: N.I., not interested.
jokes.setup: I got a LeBaron convertible right here
jokes.punchline: N.I., not interested
jokes.explanation: Pure George - turning a simple rejection into an unnecessarily formal abbreviation. Shows his character quirks.

jokes.text: Jon Voight?
jokes.setup: Previous owner was Jon Voight
jokes.punchline: George's immediate interest despite claiming N.I.
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing George's instant reversal when celebrity is involved. Economy of reaction.

jokes.text: He's a dentist. You don't wanna go out with a dentist. Why? He'll always be criticizing your brushing technique. It'll drive you crazy. Away from the gums.
jokes.setup: I have always had a big crush on Tim. Why can't he ask me out?
jokes.punchline: Away from the gums
jokes.explanation: Classic Jerry observation about dating professionals, with perfect dental advice delivery mimicking the criticism.

jokes.text: What consumer? I'm the consumer.
jokes.setup: I thought Consumer said Volvo was the car
jokes.punchline: What consumer? I'm the consumer
jokes.explanation: George misunderstanding Consumer Reports as just any consumer, showing his self-centered worldview.

jokes.text: I've never even seen him in a car. I mean, look at his movies. No cars. Deliverance, canoe. Midnight Cowboy, boots. Runaway Train... runaway train.
jokes.setup: You bought a car because it belonged to Jon Voight?
jokes.punchline: Runaway Train... runaway train
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalating list of Voight's transportation methods, ending with the perfect redundancy of 'Runaway Train... runaway train.'

jokes.text: Well, of course you do. And you know why? Because you're a bunch of yuppies. It's your 'go, go' corporate-takeover lifestyles that are driving out these mom-and-pop stores and destroying the fabric of this neighborhood.
jokes.setup: I like coffee. I like cookies.
jokes.punchline: You're a bunch of yuppies with 'go, go' corporate-takeover lifestyles
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kramer - suddenly becoming a social activist with over-the-top language like 'go, go corporate-takeover lifestyles.'

jokes.text: Well, what's so great about a mom-and-pop store? If my mom and pop ran a store, I wouldn't shop there.
jokes.setup: You're driving out these mom-and-pop stores
jokes.punchline: If my mom and pop ran a store, I wouldn't shop there
jokes.explanation: Classic Seinfeld deconstruction - taking the romantic notion of mom-and-pop stores and making it personal and realistic.

jokes.text: I really don't wear the kind of shoes that have to be cobbled.
jokes.setup: You gotta have shoes in need of a cobbler
jokes.punchline: I really don't wear the kind of shoes that have to be cobbled
jokes.explanation: Jerry's modern lifestyle clash with old-world services, delivered with characteristic precision.

jokes.text: Well, he's got a Peter Pan complex.
jokes.setup: So many sneakers
jokes.punchline: He's got a Peter Pan complex
jokes.explanation: Kramer's pseudo-psychological diagnosis of Jerry's sneaker collection as Peter Pan complex is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: You know, in the 48 years we've been here... I don't think we've ever called an electrician.
jokes.setup: You should call an electrician
jokes.punchline: In 48 years we've never called an electrician
jokes.explanation: The casual admission of 48 years of electrical neglect delivered matter-of-factly is absurdly understated.

jokes.text: No. I'm still working on it.
jokes.setup: Have you gotten all the salt off those pretzels yet?
jokes.punchline: Elaine is literally de-salting pretzels one by one
jokes.explanation: Brilliant visual gag of the world's most tedious job - removing salt from individual pretzels. Peak workplace absurdity.

jokes.text: My father was a stern man. He forbade us to participate in any activities that he thought were associated with the common man. The Thanksgiving Day Parade was first on the list.
jokes.setup: Why would you wanna hold the ropes from the Woody Woodpecker balloon?
jokes.punchline: The Thanksgiving Day Parade was first on the list of forbidden common activities
jokes.explanation: Perfect Mr. Pitt backstory explaining his elite snobbery - the parade being 'first on the list' of forbidden common activities.

jokes.text: 'Next Stop, Pottersville.' You are a genius.
jokes.setup: Elaine correctly identifies an obscure big band song
jokes.punchline: Mr. Pitt's over-the-top praise for musical knowledge
jokes.explanation: Mr. Pitt's disproportionate excitement over Elaine's musical knowledge fits his character perfectly.

jokes.text: Don't you see, that's the genius of it. If he had said Liam Neeson, you'd know he's making it up.
jokes.setup: Why would the guy make up Jon Voight?
jokes.punchline: If he said Liam Neeson, you'd know he's making it up
jokes.explanation: George's backwards logic about celebrity credibility - Jon Voight is believable precisely because he's not A-list enough to lie about.

jokes.text: Jerry, we're talking about Joe Buck. If you can play Joe Buck, Oskar Schindler's a cakewalk.
jokes.setup: How are you comparing Liam Neeson with Jon Voight?
jokes.punchline: If you can play Joe Buck, Oskar Schindler's a cakewalk
jokes.explanation: Brilliant George logic comparing acting difficulty - the absurd notion that playing a male prostitute is harder than a Holocaust hero.

jokes.text: Liam Neeson. You know he's not American.
jokes.setup: The Liam Neeson vs Jon Voight debate
jokes.punchline: Dismissing Neeson because he's not American
jokes.explanation: George's random xenophobic dismissal as if nationality determines acting ability - perfectly petty character moment.

jokes.text: Everybody's talking at me / I can't hear a word they're saying / Just driving round In Jon Voight's car
jokes.setup: George singing Midnight Cowboy theme while driving
jokes.punchline: Adapting the lyrics to reference his Jon Voight car
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback showing George fully embracing his Jon Voight fantasy by singing the Midnight Cowboy theme with modified lyrics.

jokes.text: Hey, where's all my sneakers? You said take them. Not all of them. Well, obviously, there was a miscommunication.
jokes.setup: Jerry discovers all his sneakers are gone
jokes.punchline: Kramer's casual 'obviously, there was a miscommunication'
jokes.explanation: Classic Kramer deflection - turning his overreach into a mutual 'miscommunication' with corporate-speak casualness.

jokes.text: I worked a club in Dallas one time, and they couldn't pay me... so they gave me these.
jokes.setup: Where did you get those cowboy boots?
jokes.punchline: Payment in boots from a Dallas comedy club
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedian backstory - getting paid in cowboy boots by a broke Texas club is believably absurd.

jokes.text: You look like a cowboy. I don't wanna be a cowboy.
jokes.setup: Jerry reluctantly wearing cowboy boots
jokes.punchline: Jerry's emphatic rejection of cowboy identity
jokes.explanation: Jerry's neurotic rejection of any imposed identity, even through footwear - pure character consistency.

jokes.text: Why would you call someone for addresses if you're not invited to the party? That's the genius of it.
jokes.setup: Kramer didn't get invited but Jerry got addresses
jokes.punchline: That's the genius of it - using George's earlier phrase sarcastically
jokes.explanation: Jerry sarcastically using George's earlier phrase to mock Kramer's logic about party invitations.

jokes.text: Forty-eight years, Mom. And now we have to close. All because of that idiot and his bloody nose.
jokes.setup: The shoe store faces closure due to electrical violations
jokes.punchline: Blaming Kramer's nosebleed for their 48-year electrical negligence
jokes.explanation: Perfect misplaced blame - 48 years of code violations somehow being Kramer's fault for having a nosebleed.

jokes.text: You know you got a crack in the sidewalk? You ought to get that fixed.
jokes.setup: Kramer just caused them to face closure over safety violations
jokes.punchline: Immediately pointing out another safety issue
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kramer obliviousness - having just ruined their business, he immediately spots another violation. The irony is expertly crafted.

jokes.text: Sure. That's Jon Voight's pencil. With Jon Voight's teeth marks.
jokes.setup: Finding a pencil with bite marks in the glove box
jokes.punchline: George's instant certainty it's Jon Voight's
jokes.explanation: George's desperate need to validate his purchase leads to claiming bite marks on a pencil as proof.

jokes.text: Doesn't Jon Voight spell his name J-O-N?
jokes.setup: Finding owner's manual with 'John' spelled out
jokes.punchline: Pointing out the spelling discrepancy
jokes.explanation: Perfect detail-oriented Jerry observation that deflates George's fantasy with simple logic.

jokes.text: I'm sure Jon probably misspelled his own name. I know sometimes I spell Jerry with a G. And an I.
jokes.setup: Jerry pointing out the name is spelled wrong
jokes.punchline: George claiming people misspell their own names, with absurd Jerry example
jokes.explanation: Brilliant George logic - defending his fantasy with increasingly absurd rationalizations. 'Gerry' with a G and I is perfect escalation.

jokes.text: Oh, look. There's Gregory Peck's bicycle. And Barbara Mandrell's skateboard.
jokes.setup: George kicking Jerry out for doubting the car
jokes.punchline: Jerry sarcastically spotting more celebrity vehicles
jokes.explanation: Perfect sarcastic escalation - Jerry mocking the concept by imagining other celebrity transportation with increasingly ridiculous pairings.

jokes.text: Hey, cowboy. Where's your horse? Yeah, you better run.
jokes.setup: Jerry walking in cowboy boots
jokes.punchline: Getting mocked and threatened by street toughs
jokes.explanation: Payoff to Jerry's earlier concern about looking like a cowboy - the boots actually attract unwanted attention.

jokes.text: You know, maybe his name really is J-O-H-N... but he changed it to J-O-N for show business. Well, you know, J-O-N is a lot zippier.
jokes.setup: Jerry reconsidering the name spelling issue
jokes.punchline: J-O-N is 'a lot zippier'
jokes.explanation: Jerry getting drawn into George's fantasy with show business logic - 'zippier' is a perfectly Jerry word choice.

jokes.text: I've got the entire Yankee organization at my disposal. He'll dispose of it.
jokes.setup: How would you find out about Jon Voight's name?
jokes.punchline: Playing on 'disposal' vs 'dispose of'
jokes.explanation: Mild wordplay on disposal/dispose of, showing George's confidence in his Yankees connections.

jokes.text: Which word did he emphasize? Did he say, 'Why would Jerry bring anything?' Or, 'Why would Jerry bring anything?'
jokes.setup: Analyzing Tim's response about the party
jokes.punchline: Obsessing over emphasis to determine invitation status
jokes.explanation: Perfect neurotic Jerry behavior - analyzing word emphasis like a linguistic forensics expert to decode social situations.

jokes.text: I think he emphasized 'would.'
jokes.setup: Jerry's obsessive emphasis analysis
jokes.punchline: Elaine providing the least helpful answer possible
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic timing - after Jerry's detailed analysis, Elaine picks the one word that helps nobody.

jokes.text: Mom and Pop aren't even a mom and pop? It was all an act, Jerry. They conned us, and they scored bigtime.
jokes.setup: The shoe store owners have vanished
jokes.punchline: Kramer's conspiracy theory about fake mom-and-pop identity
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Kramer paranoia - turning a simple business closure into an elaborate con job. The phrase 'scored bigtime' is perfect.

jokes.text: So Mom and Pop's plan was to move into the neighborhood... establish trust... for 48 years... and then run off with Jerry's sneakers?
jokes.setup: Kramer's conspiracy theory
jokes.punchline: Reducing it to a 48-year sneaker heist
jokes.explanation: Perfect reductio ad absurdum - breaking down Kramer's conspiracy to its logical extreme of a decades-long sneaker con. Expertly structured escalation.

jokes.text: Apparently.
jokes.setup: Jerry's sarcastic summary of the 48-year sneaker plan
jokes.punchline: Kramer's matter-of-fact acceptance
jokes.explanation: Perfect single-word response that accepts Jerry's absurd summary as completely reasonable. Maximum economy for maximum effect.

jokes.text: He finds his laugh intoxicating.
jokes.setup: Why does Mr. Pitt want to hold the Woody Woodpecker balloon?
jokes.punchline: He's intoxicated by Woody's laugh
jokes.explanation: Perfect character detail - Mr. Pitt having an oddly specific obsession with cartoon character laughter fits his eccentric personality.

jokes.text: I suppose if I had suggested Liam Neeson Day... you'd all be patting me on the back.
jokes.setup: Yankees executives rejecting Jon Voight Day
jokes.punchline: George's bitter Liam Neeson callback
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback showing George's petty resentment, bringing back the Liam Neeson comparison in a new context.

jokes.text: Hey! Jon Voight! Jon Voight! Hey, listen. Can I ask you something? Listen. Do you...? Wait...
jokes.setup: George spots Jon Voight getting into a taxi
jokes.punchline: George's awkward attempt to flag down the taxi
jokes.explanation: Classic George social awkwardness - desperately chasing a celebrity taxi with no clear plan.

jokes.text: The way I feel when I go places with you?
jokes.setup: George worried about bringing an unwelcome intruder
jokes.punchline: Jerry pointing out George is always the unwelcome one
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jerry comeback - turning George's concern back on him with characteristic precision and meanness.

jokes.text: His tooth marks. He bit me. Jon Voight bit you? What is he, a vampire?
jokes.setup: Kramer showing his arm with bite marks
jokes.punchline: Jerry's vampire comparison
jokes.explanation: Solid escalation of the Jon Voight mystery with vampire comparison adding absurdist element.

jokes.text: So you're showing up at that party with a chewed-up pencil... and Kramer's gnarled arm?
jokes.setup: George's plan to prove Jon Voight ownership
jokes.punchline: Jerry's summary of how ridiculous the evidence is
jokes.explanation: Excellent visual comedy - reducing George's detective work to its absurd physical elements.

jokes.text: Dentist? Dentist? Are you a dentist?
jokes.setup: Jerry desperately seeking dental help at the party
jokes.punchline: His awkward approach to party guests
jokes.explanation: Jerry's desperation overriding his social awareness - randomly accosting party guests for dental services.

jokes.text: Yeah. What is he, some sort of an instigator? That's right. He's a troublemaker.
jokes.setup: You got a problem with Woody Woodpecker?
jokes.punchline: Taking Woody Woodpecker's character seriously as a troublemaker
jokes.explanation: Treating a cartoon character as a real personality type - Mr. Pitt's earnest analysis of Woody as troublemaker.

jokes.text: What? I can't hear you! Where's Tim? What's that, the Empire State Building? What? I can't hear a word.
jokes.setup: Elaine's hearing damaged by loud music at deli
jokes.punchline: Her complete inability to hear anything at the party
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy payoff from earlier setup - Elaine's hearing loss creating party chaos.

jokes.text: Hey, is that Jerry Seinfeld? Hey, he didn't come with us.
jokes.setup: Jerry crashes the party
jokes.punchline: Kramer immediately disavowing Jerry
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kramer self-preservation - immediately throwing Jerry under the bus when confronted.

jokes.text: Did you say, 'Jerry, I didn't think you'd show'... or 'Jerry, I didn't think you'd show.'
jokes.setup: Tim's greeting at the party
jokes.punchline: Jerry immediately analyzing emphasis again
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback - Jerry can't help but analyze emphasis even in real-time conversation.

jokes.text: The pencil. Get the pencil out of your mouth! You're destroying Jon Voight's teeth marks.
jokes.setup: Tim examining the pencil evidence
jokes.punchline: George's panic about preserving the evidence
jokes.explanation: George's obsession with preserving his flimsy evidence creates physical comedy panic.

jokes.text: Yes. I went to dental school with him. Jon Voight, the actor? No. The periodontist.
jokes.setup: So you know Jon Voight?
jokes.punchline: The periodontist, not the actor
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection - the perfect revelation that deflates George's entire fantasy with a simple professional distinction.

jokes.text: You popped Woody Woodpecker!
jokes.setup: Jerry's dental examination at the party
jokes.punchline: Accidentally deflating the balloon during dental work
jokes.explanation: Perfect slapstick escalation - Jerry's dental emergency leads to parade sabotage.

jokes.text: Hey, who invited you anyway? You're a troublemaker.
jokes.setup: Jerry causing the balloon disaster
jokes.punchline: Calling Jerry a troublemaker like Woody Woodpecker
jokes.explanation: Nice callback connecting Jerry to the troublemaker Woody Woodpecker through his actions.

jokes.text: Especially that big kid up in the front. How old is he?
jokes.setup: Disappointed kids watching deflated Woody
jokes.punchline: Singling out an adult as a 'big kid'
jokes.explanation: Dry commentary on adult parade enthusiasm - the announcer's confusion about age-appropriate disappointment.

jokes.text: I ain't going to no Bellevue. Look at me, I'm falling apart here.
jokes.setup: Pop having severe nosebleeds needing medical attention
jokes.punchline: His stubborn refusal of help despite obvious distress
jokes.explanation: Character-consistent stubbornness - Pop's pride preventing him from seeking help despite dire situation.

jokes.text: The marching band is a perfect example of taking something bad and making it difficult too.
jokes.setup: Jerry's closing monologue about parades
jokes.punchline: Marching bands make bad things difficult
jokes.explanation: Classic Jerry observation - finding the illogical in everyday activities with perfect comedic structure.

jokes.text: Maybe if they hold still, we could all leave. Is that why they do it? People try and get away. 'No, you don't. I'm right with you. Right with you, buddy.'
jokes.setup: Why does the band have to march?
jokes.punchline: The band following escaping audience members
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation of the marching band concept - turning it into a pursuit scenario with dialogue.

jokes.text: The human urge to wave at total strangers just moving by is very strong, isn't it? Parades and ocean liners and those little trains that go through amusement parks.
jokes.setup: Jerry analyzing human behavior
jokes.punchline: Listing increasingly smaller transportation methods
jokes.explanation: Good observational setup leading to the final bit - the escalation from parades to tiny trains is well-constructed.

jokes.text: It's always that bittersweet, kind of hello-goodbye combination wave, isn't it? Just: I've never seen these people before in my life and they're leaving.
jokes.setup: The urge to wave at strangers
jokes.punchline: The absurdity of greeting people who are already leaving
jokes.explanation: Perfect ending observation - capturing the absurd poignancy of the parade wave with characteristic Seinfeld precision.

tmdb_overview: George buys a car thinking it was owned by Jon Voight; Kramer tries to save a mom-and-pop store; Elaine gets her boss into the Macy's Thanksgiving Day Parade.
